result:
The first thing I notice when I wake is the color of the light, a pale, bruised peach that means the sun is still below the horizon but has already begun its noisy broadcast. I keep my eyes shut for a moment longer, pretending I’m in charge of when the day begins, the way I used to believe I could hold my breath long enough to skip entire school afternoons. The illusion lasts three seconds, maybe four, then the radiator clanks like an old man clearing his throat and the neighbor’s dog—some enthusiastic mix of beagle and car alarm—announces that the squirrel population has not undergone overnight extinction. I open my eyes. The ceiling crack that looks like the Mississippi River has lengthened by another fingernail since October. I decide this is normal, like the way my left knee clicks ascending stairs or how every winter I forget at least one close friend’s birthday. Houses stretch, friendships contract, knees become barometers: these are the quiet laws nobody puts in the manual.

By 6:45 I’m at the kitchen window, waiting for the kettle to achieve that breathy hysteria my grandmother called “the scream of wanting.” She drank her tea in translucent glasses meant for iced drinks, the logic being that you could watch the color the way fishermen watch the horizon. I inherited none of her elegance, only the habit of staring through glass at something that isn’t there yet. Outside, the street is a black-and-white photograph someone has begun to hand-tint: a red glove lying in the slush, a lime-green trash can rolled onto its side like a drunk tourist. I imagine the glove contains an entire hand, courageous and invisible, conducting an orchestra of early-morning idling engines. The kettle screams. I pour water over mint leaves, which bruise and darken like tiny faces accepting bad news.

Years ago I read that mint is a memory herb; supposedly a leaf pressed behind the ear will make you remember your dreams. I tried it once and dreamed only of grocery aisles, fluorescent and infinite, lined with cartons of missing things. I still chew a leaf sometimes, not for dreams but for the small shock of cold, the way it reminds the tongue that sensation is a type of time travel. One bite and I’m seven, wearing a sweater knitted by the same grandmother, standing in her garden where the mint grew so aggressively it cracked the foundation. She told me plants are just the earth’s way of thinking out loud. I pictured green thoughts rising through soil, knotting themselves into stalks and blossoms, arguing with one another about which way was up. She warned me never to plant mint in open ground unless I wanted a lawn that tasted like toothpaste. I did, actually, but by then she was gone and suburbs had rules about edible landscaping.

At 7:30 I walk to the river, because motion is the easiest form of devotion. The sidewalk is a museum of last night’s dramas: a single high-heeled shoe standing upright as if its owner simply evaporated mid-stride, a smear of ketchup shaped like a country that no longer exists, the brittle skeleton of an umbrella protesting against the cruelty of wind. I greet these artifacts the way some people greet neighbors, with nods that pretend familiarity. The dog from earlier is now barking at a plastic bag flapping from a tree limb; the bag answers in the only language it owns, a soft percussive applause. Somewhere inside an apartment a baby practices opera, long vowels of outrage or discovery. I think: every sound is an autobiography.

When I reach the water, the sun has finally breached the horizon, a reckless yolk spilling across the plate of the world. Light behaves like it’s overdue on rent, bouncing off windows, ricocheting between steel bridges, sliding across the river’s skin until the whole surface looks like a single, breathing muscle. I stand there trying to feel small on purpose, the way astronauts describe seeing Earth from orbit and realizing borders are imaginary scars. Instead I feel dispersed, as though someone has taken a paintbrush to my outline. Maybe that is the same thing.

A man appears beside me carrying a metal detector, its coil embellished with stickers of planets. He doesn’t scan the sand; he scans the edge where water meets land, as though treasure favors the in-between. I want to ask what he’s looking for—wedding rings, murder weapons, subway tokens from a year when the fare was still a rumor—but he has the inward gaze of someone consulting an invisible map. After a minute he perks up, swings the detector left, right, then kneels and digs with a plastic trowel shaped like a dolphin. He extracts a bottle cap, examines it tenderly, pockets it. I imagine his house is full of such orphans: buttons, nails, a single silver earring shaped like a star. He catches me watching, lifts his eyebrows in a shrug that says the world loses things on purpose, and we are merely the night shift of reunion.

I continue upstream until the path turns away from the water and into a stand of birches. Here the snow is still etiquette-white, unspoiled by footprints or the yellow autographs of dogs. I try to step inside my own tracks, an exercise in palindromic walking, but after ten paces I misjudge and create a new angle, proving that symmetry is just a polite fiction. The trees make their small talk—woodpecker Morse, the sigh of branches remembering leaves. I place my palm on one trunk and feel the slow heartbeat. Scientists say trees emit ultrasonic clicks when thirst-stressed; I wonder what sound they make for longing. Maybe it’s pitched so low only mountains can hear, the way some whales sing for continent-sized audiences of plankton.

On the way home I pass the community garden, each plot a tiny rebellion against the concrete year. Someone has built a scarecrow from computer parts: keyboard ribcage, monitor head reflecting the sky, arms made of ethernet cables that flutter like feathers in the wind. It is both joke and prophecy. The hard drive glints with frost, a silver brain storing seasons. I think about how my own files accumulate—photos of sunsets I never looked at again, drafts of emails declaring love or surrender that I lacked the courage to send. Perhaps one day a stranger will pry open my phone the way archaeologists open tombs, fascinated by the ritual objects of digital grief.

Back inside, the radiator is still coughing. I brew more mint, leave the bag steeping until the water clouds like antique glass. On the windowsill the neighbor’s cat—an illegal tenant who has learned to jimmy the screen—sits washing its face with the solemnity of a banker counting coins. I tell it the story of the metal-detector man, the scarecrow, the glove conducting traffic. The cat blinks twice, unimpressed by anything that cannot be eaten or ignored. I decide this is wisdom: most narratives end in one of those two bins. The cat allows me one scratch behind the ears, then leaps to the fire escape, tail high like a flag of convenient alliance.

I check the clock: still morning, though the light has aged into something confident and gold. The day ahead lists itself like items on a dock: invoices, a phone call to a friend I keep forgetting, the laundry that reproduces when left unattended. They seem bearable now, ordinary spells against the enormous quiet. I open the window; cold air barges in, carrying the smell of water and motor oil, of bread beginning somewhere, of lives I will never enter. I breathe it all the way down, a tourist of atmospheres, then close the glass before the cat decides to return.

At last I sit at the table with paper and a pen that bleeds too easily, the kind of pen that insists on honesty. I write: “The first thing I notice when I wake is the color of the light,” and suddenly I am back at the river, at the garden, inside the moment when the sun paid its debt. The sentences run on like children released from school, trailing mittens and untied wonder. I let them go. Outside, the glove is still waiting for its hand, the scarecrow is still counting snowflakes, the man with the metal detector is still digging for the exact shape of loss. I write until the page looks like a map of places that almost exist, and I am the traveler who keeps arriving just after the borders dissolve.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
